prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
Here’s one: You probably overestimate how much other people notice or care about your mistakes, appearance, or social missteps.

Most of us walk around convinced that our flaws are glaringly obvious to others—whether it’s a awkward comment, a wardrobe choice, or a minor failure. In reality, people are too wrapped up in their own lives to dwell on yours. Psychologists call this the **"spotlight effect"** (we think we’re being watched more closely than we are) and the **"illusion of transparency"** (we assume our internal state is obvious to others).

The stranger writing to me might be replaying a cringe moment from years ago, certain it defined how others see them, when in fact no one remembers it. Or they might be stressing about a physical "flaw" that’s entirely invisible to the outside world.

It’s a freeing realization: you’re both less significant and more accepted than you think. (And if you *are* the rare person who doesn’t do this—well, now you know most of us are wrong about *you* too.)
